title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text: Wind resource modelling provides the probability distribution of wind states used to calculate annual production. The hydrogen configurations require more than mean wind speed, because stack efficiency and compressor operation depend on instantaneous power.

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text: Wind resource modelling provides the probability distribution of wind states used to calculate annual production. The hydrogen configurations require more than mean wind speed, because stack efficiency and compressor operation depend on instantaneous power.

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text:
Current Method
The background model describes fitting approximately ten years of offshore wind observations from the Dutch Offshore Wind Atlas to a Weibull distribution and wind rose.
For each wind state, the model evaluates turbine output and wake losses. The annual yield is then obtained by weighting the power output of each state by its probability.

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text:
Energy Balance
The wind resource page does not apply conversion losses directly. It supplies gross turbine power states to downstream components:

electricity components apply electrical efficiency and availability factors
hydrogen production evaluates stack efficiency and balance-of-plant losses at each operating point
compression power depends on hydrogen flow and pressure ratio

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text:
Source Status
The document references Dutch Offshore Wind Atlas data and a fitted wind rose. The exact data file and fitted Weibull parameters should be linked here once the model input dataset is finalised.

title: Wind Resource and Layout
text:
This section documents the wind resource, wake modelling, spacing, and infield-layout assumptions that determine gross energy production and infrastructure length.
The wind-resource and layout model connects the physical site to component sizing. Turbine positions determine wake losses and infield network geometry. Wake-aware production determines both annual yield and the operating points of hydrogen conversion equipment.

title: Wind Resource and Layout
text:
Main Dependencies

wind speed and wind direction distribution
turbine count and sea area
turbine spacing and farm power density
wake model
infield cable or pipeline topology
export distance

title: Wind Turbine
text: The wind turbine converts wind energy to mechanical and then electrical power. In the model it defines rated power, turbine count, gross production, and the capacity basis for several downstream components.

title: Wind Turbine
text: The wind turbine converts wind energy to mechanical and then electrical power. In the model it defines rated power, turbine count, gross production, and the capacity basis for several downstream components.

title: Wind Turbine
text:
Current Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Turbine rating
15 MW


Turbine CAPEX
1,100 EUR/kW


Turbine CAPEX per 15 MW turbine
16.5 million EUR


Turbine OPEX
200,000 EUR/turbine/year


Development cost
72 million EUR/farm plus 70,000 EUR/turbine



The turbine cost is treated as linear in rated power for the current 15 MW baseline.

title: Wind Turbine
text:
Energy Balance
The turbine power curve and wake model determine gross turbine output. For the electricity configuration, the turbine output measurement is treated as being after the inverter and before the internal transformer. The transformer is included in total turbine cost but its efficiency is accounted for in the electrical chain.
For decentralised hydrogen, the electrical conversion chain differs because the PEM stack can connect to the turbine DC link rather than using the full inverter-transformer path.

title: Wind Turbine
text:
Availability



Configuration
Turbine availability




Electric turbine chain
98.0%


Hydrogen turbine chain
98.5%



The hydrogen turbine availability is higher in the current assumption set because fewer turbine-side electrical conversion components are present in the decentralised hydrogen chain.

title: Wind Turbine
text:
Mass and Installation
Turbine mass is not yet fully parameterised in the source material. Tower, nacelle, and component masses should be added where they affect tower cost, foundation loading, or installation vessel requirements.

title: Turbine System
text:
The turbine system includes the wind turbine, generator, turbine-side power electronics, tower and nacelle mass, and foundation.
The turbine system is common to all architectures at the level of aerodynamic energy capture, but the downstream electrical conversion chain changes between electricity, centralised hydrogen, and decentralised hydrogen.

title: Turbine System
text:
Current Baseline
The current model uses a 15 MW turbine as the baseline unit. Turbine and foundation assumptions are documented separately because foundations, tower mass, and installation costs can scale with mass and structural loading.

title: Foundation
text: The foundation supports the offshore wind turbine and transfers turbine, tower, nacelle, and environmental loads to the seabed.

title: Foundation
text: The foundation supports the offshore wind turbine and transfers turbine, tower, nacelle, and environmental loads to the seabed.

title: Foundation
text:
Current Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Foundation type
Monopile assumption


Foundation CAPEX
6 million EUR/turbine


Foundation installation
1 million EUR/turbine


Foundation OPEX
0.5% of CAPEX per year



The current values are based on a study with TNO, according to the background documents.

title: Foundation
text:
Energy Balance
The foundation has no direct energy conversion role. Its effect enters through CAPEX, installation cost, OPEX, and potentially availability if future assumptions add foundation-related downtime.

title: Foundation
text:
Availability
No separate foundation availability factor is currently applied. Foundation-related downtime is implicitly included in the turbine or farm-level availability assumptions unless modelled otherwise.

title: Foundation
text:
Mass and Scaling
The current foundation cost is fixed per 15 MW turbine. A future refinement should connect foundation cost to turbine rating, water depth, soil condition, tower/nacelle mass, and hydrogen equipment mass where relevant.

title: Scaling Laws
text:
Several components are treated as modular and therefore scale linearly with capacity. Current examples include:

PEM stack cost
electrolyser power electronics
turbine cost at the current 15 MW baseline
linearised HVDC station cost basis

title: Scaling Laws
text:
Several components are treated as modular and therefore scale linearly with capacity. Current examples include:

PEM stack cost
electrolyser power electronics
turbine cost at the current 15 MW baseline
linearised HVDC station cost basis

title: Scaling Laws
text:
Power-Law Scaling
Some components use a power-law scaling relation:
cost = reference_cost * (capacity / reference_capacity) ^ scaling_factor
Current examples:



Component
Scaling factor




Non-electrical electrolyser BOP
0.60


Compressor
0.8335

title: Scaling Laws
text:
Discrete Scaling
Some infrastructure is discrete rather than continuously scalable:

HVDC export capacity follows 2 GW block logic in the current source framing.
Export pipelines are selected from discrete diameter and pressure options.
Stack modules are 2.5 MW units.

Discrete scaling can create optima where slightly lower yield is accepted to avoid adding another large component.

title: Financial Assumptions
text:
The model annualises capital costs using a capital recovery factor.



Parameter
Value




Weighted average cost of capital
6.6%


Project lifetime
25 years


Capital recovery factor
8.3%


Equivalent annuity period
12.09 years

title: Financial Assumptions
text:
The model annualises capital costs using a capital recovery factor.



Parameter
Value




Weighted average cost of capital
6.6%

25 years


Capital recovery factor
8.3%

title: Financial Assumptions
text:
LCOE Treatment
Annualised CAPEX, annual OPEX, and annual delivered energy are combined as:
LCOE = (annualised_CAPEX + annual_OPEX) / annual_delivered_energy
Hydrogen may also be reported in EUR/kg, but the common comparison basis is EUR/MWh using HHV.

title: Financial Assumptions
text:
Inflation and Currency
Some hydrogen component costs are derived from NREL values in 2020 USD, converted to 2025 EUR, and increased with a 50% markup to represent sales price instead of production cost.
This conversion convention should be made explicit for every imported cost source.

title: References and Source Basis
text:
To Do
Each component page should eventually link to a formal source entry with version, year, currency basis, confidentiality status, and model-use notes.

title: Platforms
text:
Platforms host offshore aggregation, conversion, and hydrogen production equipment. They affect CAPEX, installation cost, structural mass, and architecture feasibility.
The model currently uses a high-level platform cost basis from the HVDC/substation package and reuses the platform-cost assumption for centralised electrolysis where a separate platform model is not yet available.

title: Central Electrolyser Platform
text: The central electrolyser platform hosts AC aggregation, power conditioning, PEM stacks, balance of plant, compression, and pipeline export interface equipment for the centralised hydrogen architecture.

title: Central Electrolyser Platform
text: The central electrolyser platform hosts AC aggregation, power conditioning, PEM stacks, balance of plant, compression, and pipeline export interface equipment for the centralised hydrogen architecture.

title: Central Electrolyser Platform
text:
Cost Model
The current model reuses the offshore platform cost basis from the HVDC/substation package for the central electrolyser platform. Electrolyser, compressor, and pipeline costs are modelled separately.

title: Central Electrolyser Platform
text:
Energy Balance
Energy conversion on the platform includes:

transformer and rectifier losses before the stacks
stack conversion losses
BOP auxiliary consumption
purification losses
compression energy

These are documented on the hydrogen production and compressor pages.

title: Central Electrolyser Platform
text:
Availability
Availability is currently applied through the electrolyser and compressor component factors, plus any retained electrical substation availability where applicable.

title: Central Electrolyser Platform
text:
Mass and Scaling
The current source material does not yet provide a dedicated central-electrolyser platform mass model. This is a priority gap because platform mass can govern topside cost and installation cost.

title: Platform and Substation Installation
text: Platform and substation installation includes fabrication transport, heavy lifting, jacket installation, topside installation, commissioning, and offshore hookup.

title: Platform and Substation Installation
text: Platform and substation installation includes fabrication transport, heavy lifting, jacket installation, topside installation, commissioning, and offshore hookup.

title: Platform and Substation Installation
text:
Current Assumptions
For the HVDC/substation package, the current source separates:



Item
Assumption




Platform and installation portion of 2 GW package
1.5 billion EUR



This value is currently bundled and not yet decomposed into platform material, fabrication, transport, and installation.

title: Platform and Substation Installation
text:
Cost Drivers
Future decomposition should track:

topside mass
jacket mass
lift weight
installation vessel class
number of lifts
water depth
distance to shore or port
commissioning duration

title: Platform and Substation Installation
text:
Architecture Applicability
This installation category is important for electricity export and centralised hydrogen. The decentralised hydrogen architecture removes the central offshore platform in the current model definition.

title: Offshore Installation
text:
Offshore installation covers installation costs and installation drivers for turbines, foundations, cables, pipelines, platforms, and substations.
Installation costs are documented separately from component material CAPEX where the source material provides separate values. Component pages link here when installation method or vessel assumptions affect the total installed cost.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
The model boundary runs from wind turbine generation to the onshore delivery point. It includes turbines, foundations, infield collection, offshore platforms or substations, conversion equipment, export infrastructure, and onshore conversion or delivery interface.
This boundary is used because offshore electricity and offshore hydrogen differ mainly in the infrastructure required between the turbine and the shore. Excluding export infrastructure would make the comparison structurally biased toward electricity, because the electrical pathway would be evaluated before its largest carrier-specific cost items enter the calculation.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
The model boundary runs from wind turbine generation to the onshore delivery point. It includes turbines, foundations, infield collection, offshore platforms or substations, conversion equipment, export infrastructure, and onshore conversion or delivery interface.
This boundary is used because offshore electricity and offshore hydrogen differ mainly in the infrastructure required between the turbine and the shore. Excluding export infrastructure would make the comparison structurally biased toward electricity, because the electrical pathway would be evaluated before its largest carrier-specific cost items enter the calculation.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
LCOE Definition
The levelised cost of energy is calculated as annualised lifetime cost divided by annual delivered energy:
LCOE = annualised_cost / annual_energy_delivered
For electricity configurations, delivered energy is electrical energy at the onshore grid connection point. For hydrogen configurations, hydrogen yield is converted to energy using the higher heating value.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
Conceptual Cost Structure
The model uses the following conceptual decomposition to explain why infrastructure affects optimal design:
LCOE = C1 / Yield + C2 / Capacity Factor + C3
where:

C1 represents fixed project costs that do not scale strongly with installed capacity or yield.
C2 represents capacity-linked infrastructure costs, such as turbines, foundations, converters, platforms, cables, electrolysers, compressors, and pipelines.
C3 represents energy-linked costs or losses, including conversion penalties and variable operating costs.

This is a conceptual expression, not the full implementation. Its role is to show why a high cost per MW of infrastructure penalises low-capacity-factor designs: infrastructure is sized for peak power, while revenue and LCOE recovery depend on delivered annual energy.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
Annualisation
CAPEX is converted to an annual cost using a capital recovery factor. The current assumption set uses:



Parameter
Value




Weighted average cost of capital
6.6%


Project lifetime
25 years


Capital recovery factor
8.3%


Equivalent annuity period
12.09 years



OPEX is added as an annual cost, either as a fixed EUR/year value or as a percentage of component CAPEX.

title: Optimisation Variables
text: The model treats several design choices as optimisation variables rather than fixed assumptions. Their values are outcomes of the system cost minimisation.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Layout Variables
Turbine spacing and wind farm power density determine the trade-off between areal yield and capacity factor. Tighter spacing increases installed capacity per area but increases wake losses. Wider spacing improves capacity factor but lowers MWh/km2.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Hydrogen Variables
For hydrogen configurations, the main optimisation variables are:

stack overplant factor: installed electrolyser capacity relative to turbine or farm rated power
pipeline operating pressure
pipeline diameter or selected pipeline class
number of export pipelines, where pipeline capacity is discrete

The stack overplant factor affects both conversion efficiency and CAPEX. Oversizing the stack lowers current density and can improve efficiency, but increases stack CAPEX.
Pipeline pressure and diameter affect both transport capacity and compression energy. Because pipelines are discrete assets, the cost optimum may occur by accepting a small energy-yield loss if it avoids an additional pipeline.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Electricity Variables
For the electricity configuration, export capacity is constrained by HVDC platform and cable block sizing. The current model assumes a 2 GW export cable/platform basis, which creates discrete capacity increments rather than a continuously scalable export link.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Architecture-Specific Feasible Sets
Each architecture has its own feasible set:

electricity export requires AC infield collection, offshore conversion, HVDC export, and onshore conversion
centralised hydrogen keeps the AC infield network but replaces HVDC export with offshore electrolysis, compression, and hydrogen pipeline export
decentralised hydrogen moves electrolysis into the turbine and replaces the infield AC network with a hydrogen pipeline grid

title: Energy, Availability, and Annualisation
text:
Electricity yield is evaluated as delivered electrical energy at the onshore grid connection point.
Hydrogen yield is evaluated on an energy-equivalent basis using the higher heating value:



Parameter
Value




Hydrogen HHV
39.4 kWh/kg



This allows electricity and hydrogen configurations to be compared in EUR/MWh, while retaining the option to report hydrogen in EUR/kg.

title: Energy, Availability, and Annualisation
text:
Electricity yield is evaluated as delivered electrical energy at the onshore grid connection point.
Hydrogen yield is evaluated on an energy-equivalent basis using the higher heating value:



Parameter
Value




Hydrogen HHV
39.4 kWh/kg



This allows electricity and hydrogen configurations to be compared in EUR/MWh, while retaining the option to report hydrogen in EUR/kg.

title: Energy, Availability, and Annualisation
text:
Efficiency Treatment
Efficiency losses are applied at the component level. For electricity, this includes transformer, infield AC grid, offshore substation, HVDC export cable, and onshore conversion losses. For hydrogen, losses include stack conversion, balance-of-plant auxiliary demand, purification losses, power electronics where present, and compression.

title: Energy, Availability, and Annualisation
text:
Availability Treatment
Availability is applied as an annual energy multiplier, not as a time-domain repair simulation. Component unavailability is represented as a flat percentage reduction in annual yield.
The current component availability assumptions include:



Component
Availability




Turbine, electric configuration
98.0%


Infield AC grid
99.0%


Offshore substation
98.0%


HVDC export cable
99.0%


Onshore substation
98.0%


Turbine, hydrogen configuration
98.5%


Electrolyser
98.5%


Compressor
99.0%


Infield pipelines
100.0%


Export pipelines
100.0%



Pipeline availability is currently assumed not to reduce annual yield. This should be treated as a modelling convention rather than a statement that pipeline failures are impossible.

title: Water Treatment and Cooling
text: Water treatment and cooling are part of the electrolyser balance of plant. They are separated here because they can influence auxiliary power, platform footprint, water logistics, mass, and reliability.

title: Water Treatment and Cooling
text: Water treatment and cooling are part of the electrolyser balance of plant. They are separated here because they can influence auxiliary power, platform footprint, water logistics, mass, and reliability.

title: Water Treatment and Cooling
text:
Cost Model
Current cost is bundled in the non-electrical BOP cost:



Parent component
Cost basis




Non-electrical BOP
380 EUR/kW



No separate water-treatment or cooling CAPEX curve is currently documented.

title: Water Treatment and Cooling
text:
Energy Balance
Water treatment, cooling, gas handling, and purification are included in the BOP auxiliary load curve. The source material describes BOP losses as load-dependent and approximately 2% at full-load operation when purification losses are included.

title: Water Treatment and Cooling
text:
Availability
Availability is currently bundled into electrolyser availability.

title: Water Treatment and Cooling
text:
Future Refinement
If the model begins comparing centralised and decentralised seawater treatment layouts, this page should split:

desalination and purification CAPEX
cooling-system CAPEX
auxiliary power by subsystem
water consumption per kg H2
platform or turbine mass implications

title: Hydrogen Production
text:
Hydrogen production covers the PEM electrolyser stack, balance of plant, electrolyser power electronics, water treatment, cooling, and related conversion losses.
The hydrogen production model is dynamic: stack efficiency and auxiliary power consumption depend on operating point, which depends on the wind state and the installed stack capacity.

title: Hydrogen Production
text:
Current Component Cost Basis



Component
Cost at 15 MW
Scaling factor




Stack
100 EUR/kW
1.00


Non-electrical BOP
380 EUR/kW
0.60


Power electronics
87 EUR/kW
1.00



The cost basis is derived from NREL assumptions, converted from 2020 USD to 2025 EUR, with a markup applied to represent sales price rather than production cost.

title: Balance of Plant
text: Balance of plant includes non-stack auxiliary systems for hydrogen production, such as water treatment, cooling, gas handling, and hydrogen purification. Power electronics and compression are treated separately.

title: Balance of Plant
text: Balance of plant includes non-stack auxiliary systems for hydrogen production, such as water treatment, cooling, gas handling, and hydrogen purification. Power electronics and compression are treated separately.

title: Balance of Plant
text:
Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Non-electrical BOP CAPEX
380 EUR/kW


Scaling factor
0.60



The scaling factor represents economies of scale for centralised facilities. The 6/10ths rule is used: total cost scales with capacity to the power 0.6 rather than linearly.

title: Balance of Plant
text:
Energy Balance
BOP power consumption has two parts:

a fixed component independent of hydrogen production rate
a variable component that scales with hydrogen output

The BOP power demand is represented as a percentage of available electrical power as a function of operating point.
The current source material states that purification and drying lose about 1% of produced hydrogen. Total BOP-related losses are approximately 2% at full load.

title: Balance of Plant
text:
Availability
BOP availability is currently bundled into the electrolyser availability factor:



Component
Availability




Electrolyser
98.5%

title: Balance of Plant
text:
Source Basis
Power consumption data are based on Manufacturer H. The source data should be treated as confidential and represented in the model as a fitted or tabulated curve.

title: Hydrogen Infrastructure
text:
Hydrogen infrastructure includes compression, infield hydrogen pipelines, export pipelines, pressure selection, diameter selection, and pipeline capacity.
Compression and pipeline assumptions are coupled: higher pipeline inlet pressure increases compression energy and compressor cost, but also increases pipeline capacity and can reduce the number of parallel export pipelines.
The current hydrogen infrastructure model assumes no additional hydrogen losses in pipelines after compression.

title: Compressor
text:
The compressor raises hydrogen from electrolyser outlet pressure to the required pipeline inlet pressure. It is present in centralised and decentralised hydrogen architectures.
PEM stack outlet pressure is assumed to be about 30 bar. Pipeline inlet pressure is an optimisation variable, with examples up to 150 bar.

title: Compressor
text:
The compressor raises hydrogen from electrolyser outlet pressure to the required pipeline inlet pressure. It is present in centralised and decentralised hydrogen architectures.
PEM stack outlet pressure is assumed to be about 30 bar. Pipeline inlet pressure is an optimisation variable, with examples up to 150 bar.

title: Compressor
text:
Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Compressor CAPEX at 15 MW, 30 to 150 bar
56 EUR/kW


Scaling factor
0.8335


Source basis
Transition Accelerator



Compressor cost scales mainly with electrical compressor power, which depends on specific compression energy and hydrogen flow rate.

title: Compressor
text:
Energy Balance
Compression work is modelled as a staged isothermal compression process. The maximum compression ratio per stage is assumed to be 3.3. Higher total ratios require multiple stages, and each stage is assumed to use the same compression ratio.
Current assumptions:



Parameter
Value




Hydrogen specific heat ratio
1.41


Gas temperature
298 K


Isothermal efficiency
80%


Motor efficiency
95%



The model uses the average compressibility factor between inlet and outlet conditions.

title: Compressor
text:
Availability



Component
Availability




Compressor
99.0%

title: Compressor
text:
Installation and Mass
Compressor installation is included in the associated turbine or central platform architecture. A component mass model should be added if compressor mass affects turbine tower, platform, or lifting assumptions.

title: Onshore Grid Connection
text: The onshore grid connection receives exported HVDC power and converts it to AC at the national high-voltage grid interface.

title: Infield AC Cables
text: Infield AC cables collect power from turbines and deliver it to an offshore substation or centralised hydrogen platform. They are used in the electricity and centralised hydrogen architectures.

Installation is modelled per metre. See Cable Installation for vessel and benchmark assumptions.

title: Infield AC Cables
text:
Sizing Drivers
The number of strings depends on turbine rating and cable capacity. For 15 MW turbines and 70 MW cable capacity, only a limited number of turbines can be placed on each string.

title: HVDC Converter Stations
text: HVDC converter stations convert offshore AC power to HVDC for export and convert HVDC back to AC onshore. They are used in the electricity export architecture.

title: HVDC Converter Stations
text: HVDC converter stations convert offshore AC power to HVDC for export and convert HVDC back to AC onshore. They are used in the electricity export architecture.

title: Electricity Export with HVDC
text: The electricity architecture is the reference case. Wind turbine output is conditioned for AC infield collection, aggregated offshore, converted to HVDC for export, and converted back to AC at shore.

title: Electricity Export with HVDC
text: The electricity architecture is the reference case. Wind turbine output is conditioned for AC infield collection, aggregated offshore, converted to HVDC for export, and converted back to AC at shore.

title: Electricity Export with HVDC
text:
Component Chain

turbine generator
turbine rectifier, inverter, and transformer
66 kV AC infield cable network
offshore AC substation and HVDC converter platform
HVDC export cable
onshore converter station and grid connection

title: Electricity Export with HVDC
text:
Modelling Implications
The key modelling feature is that export infrastructure is sized for peak installed capacity. HVDC platforms and export cables therefore contribute strongly to the capacity-linked cost term.
The current assumption set uses a 2 GW HVDC export basis. This creates discrete export capacity increments and makes under-utilisation of peak-sized infrastructure important in the LCOE calculation.

title: About
text:
This site documents the assumptions behind a component-level techno-economic model for offshore wind infrastructure.
The model compares electricity-based export, centralised offshore hydrogen production, and decentralised turbine-integrated hydrogen production. The documentation here is limited to methodology and assumptions: system boundaries, component cost models, energy balances, availability factors, installation assumptions, and shared financial conventions.
The source material is a set of working Word documents in background/. Some assumptions rely on internal supplier or manufacturer data; those are documented at the level needed for model reproducibility without exposing confidential raw datasets.

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text: The centralised hydrogen architecture keeps the conventional wind turbine and AC infield collection system up to the offshore aggregation point. At the offshore platform, electricity is converted to hydrogen using electrolysers, compressed, and exported through a subsea hydrogen pipeline.

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text: The centralised hydrogen architecture keeps the conventional wind turbine and AC infield collection system up to the offshore aggregation point. At the offshore platform, electricity is converted to hydrogen using electrolysers, compressed, and exported through a subsea hydrogen pipeline.

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text:
Component Chain

turbine generator
turbine rectifier, inverter, and transformer
66 kV AC infield cable network
offshore substation and central electrolyser platform
electrolyser power electronics
PEM stacks and balance of plant
compressor
export hydrogen pipeline
onshore hydrogen backbone connection

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text:
Modelling Implications
This architecture replaces the HVDC converter platform and HVDC export cable with a central electrolyser, compressor, and hydrogen pipeline. It retains many electrical infield assumptions, but changes the export infrastructure and introduces conversion losses.
The central facility can benefit from economies of scale in the non-electrical balance of plant. Stack and power electronics costs are treated as modular and scale approximately linearly.

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text:
Related Components

Balance of Plant
PEM Electrolyser Stack
Compressor
Hydrogen Pipelines

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text: The decentralised architecture places electrolysis inside the turbine. Energy is converted to hydrogen as close as possible to generation, and the infield network becomes a hydrogen pipeline grid instead of an AC cable grid.

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text: The decentralised architecture places electrolysis inside the turbine. Energy is converted to hydrogen as close as possible to generation, and the infield network becomes a hydrogen pipeline grid instead of an AC cable grid.

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text:
Component Chain

turbine generator
turbine-side rectification or DC link interface
PEM stacks installed in or at the turbine
local balance of plant
turbine-level compression to infield pressure
infield hydrogen pipeline grid
export hydrogen pipeline
onshore hydrogen backbone connection

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text:
Modelling Implications
This architecture removes several electrical conversion steps. In the centralised and electricity configurations, turbine output is converted from variable-frequency AC to DC, then to grid-quality AC, then stepped up for infield collection. For central electrolysis, the power is later stepped down and rectified again before entering the stack.
In the decentralised configuration, the stack connects more directly to the turbine DC link. The model therefore excludes the turbine inverter/step-up transformer and the platform-side step-down transformer/rectifier that would otherwise be needed for electrolysis.
The infield grid is also structurally different. A 66 kV AC string is capacity-limited to roughly 60-70 MW, while a 6 inch hydrogen pipeline at 150 bar is assumed to carry roughly 500 MW equivalent. This removes the electrical string capacity constraint from the infield design.

title: Architectures
text:
The model compares three offshore wind export architectures:
The architectures differ in where energy conversion occurs and which infrastructure carries energy to shore.

title: Architectures
text:
Architecture Logic
The electrical architecture transports electrons to shore. The hydrogen architectures convert wind power to hydrogen offshore and transport gas to shore. The centralised hydrogen architecture converts at an offshore platform. The decentralised hydrogen architecture converts inside each turbine and uses an infield hydrogen grid.
The model treats these architectures as different feasible sets. Components are not swapped one-for-one; changing the carrier changes the cost, loss, availability, and installation structure of the whole value chain.

title: HVDC Export Cables
text: HVDC export cables transport electrical energy from the offshore converter station to the onshore converter station.

title: HVDC Export Cables
text: HVDC export cables transport electrical energy from the offshore converter station to the onshore converter station.

title: HVDC Export Cables
text:
Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Export cable capacity
2 GW


Export cables installed in parallel
1


Installed cost benchmark
7 million EUR/km


Installation cost
635 EUR/m


Implied cable CAPEX
6,365 EUR/m


OPEX
2% of CAPEX per year



The material CAPEX is derived by subtracting the modelled installation cost from the installed-cost benchmark.

title: HVDC Export Cables
text:
Energy Balance



Component
Efficiency




HVDC export cable
99.5%

title: HVDC Export Cables

Availability



Component
Availability




HVDC export cable

title: HVDC Export Cables
text:
Installation
Cable installation is modelled separately from material CAPEX. See Cable Installation.

title: HVDC Export Cables
text:
Sizing Drivers
Export cable sizing is driven by rated export capacity and distance to shore. The current baseline distance to shore is 80 km.

title: Offshore AC Substation
text: The offshore AC substation aggregates the infield AC network. In the electricity architecture, it forms part of the path toward HVDC conversion. In the centralised hydrogen architecture, it feeds the offshore electrolyser facility.

title: Offshore AC Substation
text: The offshore AC substation aggregates the infield AC network. In the electricity architecture, it forms part of the path toward HVDC conversion. In the centralised hydrogen architecture, it feeds the offshore electrolyser facility.

title: Offshore AC Substation
text:
Cost Model
The offshore substation cost is currently bundled with the HVDC station assumptions where the source refers to a 2 GW offshore station package. For centralised hydrogen, the same platform cost basis is used for the central electrolyser platform unless refined separately.

title: Offshore AC Substation
text:
Energy Balance



Component
Efficiency




Offshore substation
98.5%

title: Offshore AC Substation
text:
Availability



Component
Availability




Offshore substation
98.0%

title: Offshore AC Substation
text:
Installation and Mass
Substation installation is treated with platform and substation installation assumptions. See Platform and Substation Installation.
The source material does not yet provide a separate mass-scaling model for the AC substation.

title: Electrical Infrastructure
text:
Electrical infrastructure covers the AC infield network, offshore AC substation, HVDC converter stations, export cable, and onshore grid connection.
These components are central to the electricity export architecture and partially retained in the centralised hydrogen architecture. They are largely removed from the decentralised hydrogen architecture.

title: Electrical Infrastructure
text:
Current Efficiency Assumptions



Component
Efficiency




Transformer
99.5%


Infield AC grid
98.0%


Offshore substation
98.5%


HVDC export cable
99.5%


Onshore substation
To be confirmed

title: Electrical Infrastructure
text:
Current Availability Assumptions



Component
Availability




Infield AC grid
99.0%


Offshore substation
98.0%


HVDC export cable
99.0%


Onshore substation
98.0%

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Hydrogen pipelines transport compressed hydrogen through the infield grid and from the wind farm to shore.
The model distinguishes between infield pipelines and export pipelines, but documents them together because the same capacity, pressure, material, and installation logic applies.

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Hydrogen pipelines transport compressed hydrogen through the infield grid and from the wind farm to shore.
The model distinguishes between infield pipelines and export pipelines, but documents them together because the same capacity, pressure, material, and installation logic applies.

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Infield pipeline installation
635 EUR/m


Infield pipeline OPEX
0.5% of CAPEX per year


Export pipelines installed in parallel
3


Export pipeline installation
635 EUR/m


Export pipeline OPEX
0.5% of CAPEX per year



Pipeline material CAPEX is based on confidential supplier data. The current model uses cost data for selected diameter and pressure combinations and fits a two-dimensional regression to interpolate or extrapolate.

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Capacity Model
Pipeline capacity depends on:

diameter
inlet pressure
outlet pressure
route length
roughness and friction parameters

The current export-pipeline calculation uses the Colebrook equation from Perry’s Chemical Engineers’ Handbook, with Darcy-Weisbach roughness and Hazen-Williams friction factors from the manufacturer.

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Current Sizing Assumptions



Parameter
Value




Baseline export length
80 km


Export pipeline outlet pressure
66 bar


Maximum composite pipeline diameter
8 inch


Infield pipeline diameter
6 inch


Infield pipeline pressure
150 bar


Infield pipeline capacity
about 500 MW equivalent



The 66 bar outlet pressure is selected so hydrogen can be injected into the expected Dutch hydrogen backbone without an additional compressor.

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Energy Balance
The current model assumes no additional hydrogen losses in the pipelines after compression. Pipeline pressure drop affects required compression and capacity, not a direct hydrogen loss term.

title: Hydrogen Pipelines
text:
Availability



Component
Availability




Infield pipelines
100%


Export pipelines
100%



This is a modelling convention and should be tested in sensitivity work if pipeline outages become material.

title: Pipeline Pressure and Capacity
text: Pipeline pressure links compressor energy, compressor CAPEX, pipeline capacity, and number of parallel pipelines.

title: Pipeline Pressure and Capacity
text: Pipeline pressure links compressor energy, compressor CAPEX, pipeline capacity, and number of parallel pipelines.

title: Pipeline Pressure and Capacity
text:
Optimisation Logic
Higher inlet pressure increases the capacity of a given pipeline but increases compression work. Larger diameters reduce cost per unit capacity but are limited by installation and flexibility constraints.
The source material notes that the cost optimum often prefers the largest feasible diameter because it has the lowest cost per capacity. However, because pipelines come in discrete units, they can be over-dimensioned. Reducing pressure can then lower compression cost while retaining enough capacity.

title: Pipeline Pressure and Capacity
text:
Capacity Equation Inputs
The capacity calculation uses:

pipeline diameter
inlet pressure
outlet pressure
pipeline length
gas density and compressibility
surface roughness
friction relation

title: Pipeline Pressure and Capacity
text:
Model Outputs
For each pipeline option, the model calculates:

maximum hydrogen mass flow
MW-equivalent transport capacity
required number of parallel export pipelines
compressor power needed to reach inlet pressure
annualised pipeline and compressor cost contribution

title: Electrolyser Power Electronics
text: Electrolyser power electronics condition electrical power before it enters the PEM stack. The need for these components differs between centralised and decentralised hydrogen.

title: Electrolyser Power Electronics
text: Electrolyser power electronics condition electrical power before it enters the PEM stack. The need for these components differs between centralised and decentralised hydrogen.

title: Electrolyser Power Electronics
text:
Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Power electronics CAPEX
87 EUR/kW


Scaling factor
1.00



Power electronics are treated as mature, modular components and do not receive central-facility economies of scale.

title: Electrolyser Power Electronics
text:
Energy Balance
For centralised electrolysis, the infield AC power is stepped down and rectified to DC before entering the stack. Current explicit losses are:



Component
Loss




Rectifier
1.0%


Transformer
0.5%



For decentralised electrolysis, these platform-side components are absent because the stack connects to the turbine DC link.

title: Electrolyser Power Electronics
text:
Availability
Availability is currently included in the electrolyser availability factor unless separated in a later version.

title: Electrolyser Power Electronics
text:
Architecture Applicability







Architecture
Treatment




Centralised hydrogen
Included


Decentralised hydrogen
Excluded or strongly reduced due to DC-link integration


Electricity export
Covered by turbine and HVDC electrical infrastructure pages

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
The PEM stack converts electrical power to hydrogen. It is present in both centralised and decentralised hydrogen architectures.
PEM is selected because it can follow fluctuating wind power and produces hydrogen at elevated outlet pressure, approximately 30 bar in the source material. This reduces the downstream compression ratio compared with low-pressure hydrogen production.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
The PEM stack converts electrical power to hydrogen. It is present in both centralised and decentralised hydrogen architectures.
PEM is selected because it can follow fluctuating wind power and produces hydrogen at elevated outlet pressure, approximately 30 bar in the source material. This reduces the downstream compression ratio compared with low-pressure hydrogen production.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Cost Model



Item
Assumption




Stack CAPEX
100 EUR/kW


Scaling factor
1.00


Module size
2.5 MW


Minimum operating load per module
250 kW


Rated current density
3 A/cm2



Stack costs are treated as modular and linear with installed capacity. The source material states that centralised facilities do not receive additional stack economies of scale because stacks are already MW-scale modules; larger facilities mainly number-up modules.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Energy Balance
Stack efficiency is calculated from a manufacturer polarisation curve relating cell voltage to current density. The operating point is defined as a percentage of maximum stack power or maximum current density.
Oversizing the stack lowers current density for a given power input and can improve efficiency, but increases CAPEX. The model therefore treats stack overplanting as an optimisation variable.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Module Switching
The model does not force all modules to operate simultaneously. At each wind state, it selects the number of active modules that maximises conversion efficiency while respecting minimum load. This allows hydrogen production at low turbine output without operating a stack below the minimum safe operating threshold.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Availability



Component
Availability




Electrolyser
98.5%

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Installation and Mass
Stack installation is included in the electrolyser facility or turbine-integrated hydrogen system. A separate stack mass model is not yet documented and should be added if stack mass affects tower, platform, or installation cost.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Source Basis
Stack cost assumptions are based on NREL-derived cost data and manufacturer efficiency data. The manufacturer polarisation curve is treated as confidential source data.

title: Offshore Wind Infrastructure Model Assumptions
text:
This encyclopedia documents the methodology and techno-economic assumptions used to model offshore wind infrastructure, electricity export, offshore hydrogen production, and hydrogen transport.
The site is organised as an assumptions manual. Each component page bundles the modelling choices that matter for that component:
The emphasis is deliberately methodological. Results, scenario outcomes, and interpretation of model output are out of scope for this encyclopedia.

title: Offshore Wind Infrastructure Model Assumptions
text:
Main Sections

Methodology
Architectures
Wind Resource and Layout
Turbine System
Electrical Infrastructure
Hydrogen Production
Hydrogen Infrastructure
Platforms
Offshore Installation
Shared Model Inputs

title: Modelling Workflow
text: The model workflow separates fixed assumptions, scenario parameters, derived parameters, optimisation variables, and outputs.

title: Modelling Workflow
text:
Fixed Inputs
Fixed inputs remain constant across the compared architectures unless explicitly varied in a sensitivity case. Examples include:

wind resource and Weibull distributions
distance to shore
water depth
component cost assumptions
efficiency and availability assumptions
financing parameters
source-specific cost curves

title: Modelling Workflow
text:
Scenario Parameters
Scenario parameters define the case being evaluated. Current scenario dimensions include:

sea area
number of turbines
export architecture: electricity, centralised hydrogen, or decentralised hydrogen
hydrogen stack overplanting
hydrogen pipeline pressure
hydrogen pipeline selection

title: Modelling Workflow
text:
Derived Parameters
Derived parameters are calculated deterministically from fixed inputs and scenario parameters. Examples include:

turbine positions
infield cable or pipeline length
wake losses
production profile
stack operating point and efficiency
balance-of-plant losses
compressor power demand
peak export capacity requirement

title: Modelling Workflow
text:
Outputs
The principal outputs are yield and LCOE. The encyclopedia does not document result values, but it documents the assumptions needed to reproduce them:

annual delivered energy in MWh/year
areal yield in MWh/km2/year
LCOE in EUR/MWh or EUR/kg-equivalent reporting where needed
component-level annualised cost contribution
optimised component sizing decisions

title: Methodology
text:
This section defines the modelling logic used across all component pages. The model is a deterministic techno-economic optimisation framework for offshore wind systems with alternative energy carriers.
The modelling object is the complete turbine-to-onshore-delivery value chain. The purpose is not to compare isolated component prices, but to represent how component cost, energy conversion, availability, and installation assumptions interact when the energy carrier changes from electricity to hydrogen.

title: Methodology
text:
Methodological Role
The methodology pages define conventions that are shared across components:

the system boundary used for cost and yield accounting
the LCOE objective function
the modelling workflow from fixed inputs to derived parameters and outputs
the optimisation variables treated as model outcomes
the annualisation, energy-basis, and availability conventions used in component calculations

Component-specific assumptions are not collected here. They are documented on the relevant component pages.

title: Methodology
text:
Core Principle
Infrastructure is modelled as part of the offshore energy system, not as an external connection. This matters because export infrastructure is sized for peak capacity but paid back through delivered annual energy. The cost of capacity therefore changes the economically optimal layout and carrier choice.

title: Cable Installation
text: Cable installation covers laying, burial, pull-in, mobilisation, and other installation activities for infield and export electrical cables.

title: Cable Installation
text: Cable installation covers laying, burial, pull-in, mobilisation, and other installation activities for infield and export electrical cables.

title: Cable Installation
text:
Current Assumptions



Item
Assumption




Infield cable installation
635 EUR/m


Export cable installation
635 EUR/m



The source material also cites an installer benchmark for Vanguard East and West: 192 turbines, about 3 GW total, 452 km of infield cabling, and 600 million EUR in cabling installation-related cost. This corresponds to roughly 1,300 EUR/m or about 200 EUR/kW of wind farm capacity.
The current model value of 635 EUR/m should therefore be treated as a specific modelling assumption that may need reconciliation with the benchmark once scope is finalised.

title: Cable Installation
text:
Cost Drivers
Installation cost depends on:

route length
number of cable strings
number of pull-ins
burial requirements
vessel day rate
laying speed
crossing and routing complexity

title: Cable Installation
text:
Architecture Applicability
Cable installation applies to electricity export and centralised hydrogen architectures. It is largely removed from decentralised hydrogen except for any residual electrical systems.

title: Pipeline Installation
text: Pipeline installation covers laying and connecting infield and export hydrogen pipelines.

title: Pipeline Installation
text: Pipeline installation covers laying and connecting infield and export hydrogen pipelines.

title: Pipeline Installation
text:
Current Assumptions



Item
Assumption




Infield pipeline installation
635 EUR/m


Export pipeline installation
635 EUR/m



The model notes that a single vessel may be able to lay three pipelines simultaneously, while only one cable is laid at a time. This can lower installation cost per metre for pipelines relative to cables when scope is defined consistently.

title: Pipeline Installation
text:
Cost Drivers
Pipeline installation depends on:

pipeline diameter
pipeline material
spooling feasibility
number of parallel pipelines
route length
burial or protection requirements
connection and termination count

title: Pipeline Installation
text:
Material Link
Composite pipelines are preferred in the source material for relevant infield diameters because they are lighter, can be spooled, and can simplify installation compared with steel.

title: Turbine and Foundation Installation
text: Turbine and foundation installation converts component supply cost into installed offshore asset cost.

title: Turbine and Foundation Installation
text: Turbine and foundation installation converts component supply cost into installed offshore asset cost.

title: Turbine and Foundation Installation
text:
Current Assumptions







Item
Assumption




Foundation installation
1 million EUR/turbine


Turbine installation
Included in current turbine/foundation assumption set unless separated later



The current source material provides a separate foundation installation cost but does not yet provide a full vessel-based turbine installation model.

title: Turbine and Foundation Installation
text:
Cost Drivers
Future installation modelling should account for:

turbine rating
turbine mass and hub height
foundation mass
water depth
distance to port
weather downtime
vessel day rate
installation duration

title: Turbine and Foundation Installation
text:
Related Components

Wind Turbine
Foundation
Tower, Nacelle, and Mass

title: HVDC and Substation Platforms
text: HVDC and substation platforms support offshore electrical conversion and aggregation equipment.

title: HVDC and Substation Platforms
text: HVDC and substation platforms support offshore electrical conversion and aggregation equipment.

title: HVDC and Substation Platforms
text:
Cost Model
The current source basis is a TenneT 2 GW station package:



Item
Assumption




Total 2 GW offshore station package
3.5 billion EUR


Substation/converter component
2.0 billion EUR


Platform and installation component
1.5 billion EUR


Linearised total cost basis
1,750 EUR/kW



The model currently assumes linear scaling with capacity.

title: HVDC and Substation Platforms
text:
Energy Balance
The platform itself has no direct energy conversion efficiency. Electrical equipment hosted on the platform is covered in the electrical infrastructure pages.

title: HVDC and Substation Platforms
text:
Availability
Platform-related availability is bundled into offshore substation availability unless separated later.

title: HVDC and Substation Platforms
text:
Mass and Installation
Platform mass is not yet explicitly parameterised. The platform and installation cost component should eventually be split into jacket/topside mass, lifting requirements, vessel spread, and installation duration.

title: Platform Mass and Scaling
text: Platform mass can govern jacket cost, topside cost, installation method, and heavy-lift vessel requirements.

title: Platform Mass and Scaling
text: Platform mass can govern jacket cost, topside cost, installation method, and heavy-lift vessel requirements.

title: Platform Mass and Scaling
text:
Current Status
The current assumption set uses aggregate platform cost values rather than explicit mass-based scaling. This is acceptable for a first-pass techno-economic model but limits the ability to compare platform-heavy centralised hydrogen against HVDC platforms in detail.

title: Platform Mass and Scaling
text:
Future Mass Model Requirements
A platform mass model should document:

topside equipment mass by component
jacket or support-structure mass
installation lift weight
number of lifts
vessel class
mass-cost scaling exponent
availability or maintainability effects where relevant

title: Platform Mass and Scaling
text:
Architecture Relevance







Architecture
Platform relevance




Electricity export
HVDC converter and substation platform


Centralised hydrogen
Electrolyser, BOP, compression, and export interface platform


Decentralised hydrogen
No central offshore platform in the current configuration

title: CAPEX and OPEX Conventions
text:
CAPEX is documented as either:

a unit cost, such as EUR/kW or EUR/m
a fixed cost per turbine or farm
a fitted cost curve
a capacity-scaling relation

Where installation is separately available, component CAPEX and installation cost are documented separately.

title: CAPEX and OPEX Conventions
text:
CAPEX is documented as either:

a unit cost, such as EUR/kW or EUR/m
a fixed cost per turbine or farm
a fitted cost curve
a capacity-scaling relation

Where installation is separately available, component CAPEX and installation cost are documented separately.

title: CAPEX and OPEX Conventions
text:
OPEX
OPEX is represented as either:

fixed EUR/year
EUR/unit/year
percentage of CAPEX per year

Current examples:



Component
OPEX assumption




Wind turbine
200,000 EUR/turbine/year


Foundation
0.5% of CAPEX/year


Infield AC cable
2% of CAPEX/year


Export cable
2% of CAPEX/year


Infield pipeline
0.5% of CAPEX/year


Export pipeline
0.5% of CAPEX/year

title: CAPEX and OPEX Conventions
text:
Installation Cost
Installation cost is included with component CAPEX only where the source provides installed cost and no split is available. Otherwise, installation is documented separately.

title: Shared Model Inputs
text:
Shared model inputs define conventions used by many component pages. They do not replace component-level documentation; they prevent repeated explanation of common modelling rules.
Shared inputs include financial assumptions, CAPEX/OPEX conventions, scaling laws, source-quality labels, and uncertainty treatment.

title: Source Quality and Uncertainty
text:
Assumptions in this encyclopedia come from several source types:

public literature or reports
supplier or manufacturer data
internal engineering estimates
benchmark projects
model-derived fitted curves

title: Source Quality and Uncertainty
text:
Assumptions in this encyclopedia come from several source types:

public literature or reports
supplier or manufacturer data
internal engineering estimates
benchmark projects
model-derived fitted curves

title: Source Quality and Uncertainty
text:
Confidential Sources
Some assumptions rely on confidential source data, including manufacturer stack and BOP data and supplier pipeline cost data. These should be documented by method and model representation without publishing raw confidential tables.

title: Source Quality and Uncertainty
text:
Uncertainty Treatment
Where source uncertainty is material, the model should document:

base value
uncertainty range
sensitivity range
reason for uncertainty
affected model outputs

The background model currently identifies pipeline material cost, electrolyser cost, and infrastructure cost assumptions as important candidates for sensitivity analysis.

title: Source Quality and Uncertainty
text:
Documentation Status Labels
Recommended status labels:

implemented: assumption is used in the model and documented here
to verify: assumption is in the drafts but source needs final verification
placeholder: component exists in the value chain but quantitative assumption is incomplete
confidential: model uses data that should not be reproduced directly

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text: The generator produces variable-frequency AC power. Power electronics condition this output for either AC grid injection or electrolysis.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text: The generator produces variable-frequency AC power. Power electronics condition this output for either AC grid injection or electrolysis.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text:
Electricity and Centralised Hydrogen Chain
For conventional electrical operation, the turbine output is rectified to DC, inverted to grid-quality AC, and stepped up by a transformer to the infield grid voltage.
Centralised hydrogen uses the same turbine-side chain up to the infield AC network. At the offshore platform, power is stepped down and rectified again before entering the electrolyser stacks.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text:
Decentralised Hydrogen Chain
In the decentralised architecture, electrolysers are placed inside or at the turbine and can connect to the turbine DC link. The inverter and step-up transformer on the turbine side are not required for the hydrogen pathway, and the platform-side step-down transformer and rectifier are absent.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text:
Energy Balance
Current explicit assumptions include:



Conversion step
Efficiency / loss




Central electrolyser rectifier
1.0% loss


Central electrolyser transformer
0.5% loss


Turbine transformer in electrical chain
99.5% efficiency



Other converter losses should be added once the model source table is finalised.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text:
Cost Model
Turbine-side power electronics are included in the turbine system cost where they are part of the standard turbine. Electrolyser power electronics are documented separately in Electrolyser Power Electronics.

title: Tower, Nacelle, and Mass
text:
Mass assumptions matter when component placement changes tower loading, nacelle design, foundation requirements, and installation cost.
This page is reserved for mass descriptions that affect the techno-economic model. The most important future addition is the difference between conventional turbine electrical equipment and turbine-integrated electrolysis equipment in the decentralised hydrogen architecture.

title: Tower, Nacelle, and Mass
text:
Mass assumptions matter when component placement changes tower loading, nacelle design, foundation requirements, and installation cost.
This page is reserved for mass descriptions that affect the techno-economic model. The most important future addition is the difference between conventional turbine electrical equipment and turbine-integrated electrolysis equipment in the decentralised hydrogen architecture.

title: Tower, Nacelle, and Mass
text:
Current Status
The background documents identify mass as relevant, but do not yet provide a complete mass model for:

tower mass
nacelle mass
in-turbine electrolyser mass
compressor mass
transformer and converter mass
structural reinforcement

title: Tower, Nacelle, and Mass
text:
Modelling Treatment
Until explicit mass functions are added, turbine CAPEX, foundation CAPEX, and installation cost are represented through fixed component assumptions. Any future mass-linked correction should state:

mass driver
affected component cost
scaling relation
source basis
architecture applicability

title: Infield Layout
text: Infield layout determines the length and topology of the collection network between turbines and the offshore aggregation or export point. The model distinguishes between electrical cable strings and hydrogen pipeline ladders.

title: Infield Layout
text: Infield layout determines the length and topology of the collection network between turbines and the offshore aggregation or export point. The model distinguishes between electrical cable strings and hydrogen pipeline ladders.

title: Infield Layout
text:
Electrical Infield Layout
The electrical infield network is constrained by cable capacity. The current assumption set uses a 66 kV AC infield cable capacity of 70 MW. Large farms therefore require multiple strings, each connecting only a limited number of turbines.
The model estimates average string length from farm geometry rather than solving a detailed cable-routing optimisation. The average string length is approximated from the distance between the farm centre and corner. An additional 20% is added to represent diversions and crossing constraints.

title: Infield Layout
text:
Hydrogen Infield Layout
The hydrogen infield network is not constrained in the same way. The current assumption assumes 6 inch infield pipelines at 150 bar with about 500 MW equivalent capacity. This enables a ladder topology where each turbine has two possible routes to the export pipeline.
For a square layout, the ladder length is estimated from the side length of the farm and the number of rungs, which scales with the square root of turbine count.

title: Infield Layout
text:
Cost and Installation Link
Infield layout feeds directly into component cost pages:

Infield AC Cables
Hydrogen Pipelines
Cable Installation
Pipeline Installation

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text: Wake modelling translates turbine layout into gross and wake-corrected energy yield. Turbine spacing is the main design variable connecting energy production to infrastructure economics.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text: Wake modelling translates turbine layout into gross and wake-corrected energy yield. Turbine spacing is the main design variable connecting energy production to infrastructure economics.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text:
Current Method
The model uses PyWake to construct a wake-aware power lookup table indexed by wind speed and direction. Turbine positions are arranged on a rectangular grid scaled to the sea area and turbine count.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text:
Yield-Capacity Factor Trade-off
Tighter spacing increases installed capacity per km2 and can raise total areal yield. It also increases wake losses and lowers the capacity factor. Wider spacing reduces wake losses and improves capacity factor, but uses more area per MW.
This trade-off is architecture-dependent because capacity-linked infrastructure costs differ across electricity and hydrogen configurations.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text:
Cost Link
Spacing affects:

number of turbines for a fixed area and density
wake losses and annual energy yield
capacity factor
infield cable or pipeline length
utilisation of peak-sized export infrastructure

The model therefore treats spacing or wind farm power density as a design variable, not merely a site descriptor.
